sentences.text: Yes, we were sitting in the compartment, and oh, of course mother had-- I was also to meet-- my aunt from Holland was also going to see me before I left the country.
And my mother had given me a few pieces of jewelry that had been left to give to this aunt in the hopes that she would be able to trade for food, shall we say, or for anything they needed.
Because she, this aunt had been joined by another sister, also with children.
The one from Frankfurt had gone to Holland hoping that they would be safer there.
And we rode on the train all day.
We did not see-- I didn't see anybody on the train as far as chaperones.
I do know that they were supposed to be on the train because there were chaperones who came with us but had to return to Germany.
That was a stipulation.
If they did not return, the transports would stop.
And so we were almost in Holland, and suddenly my name was called out that my luggage was to be examined.
Whether it was because this aunt had to get permission to go on the train or whether it was just random, I don't know.
However, I had to hide those few pieces of jewelry.
And that's why I told you in the beginning that my mother had bought me the pen, because that became a little hiding place.
Remember, I was eight years old.
I still say I was eight going on 45 at that point.
I took out the pen and I dropped the necklace into the little case and put the pen back in neatly.
I found another hiding place in-- this I thought of, too-- was I put two other necklaces down my hosiery, down my stockings, so that it would land in the instep of my shoes.
And so when they turned my suitcases upside down and they searched them, there was nothing there but clothing and the couple of things that my father had given me, such as my personal pictures.
He had given me a little alarm clock.
You know, that kind of thing, but they found nothing else.
And we finally reached the border of Holland, and my aunt came on board the train.
And I had to retrieve these things.
And somehow, we managed it.
We then went on to the hook of Holland, and we were there probably around midnight.
And there we were loaded onto boats.
My fond name for them was bathtubs.
We single filed off the train, and as randomly as we got off two to a cabin, whoever you happened to be with.
I was in a cabin with a six-year-old, and I was eight so I was put in charge.

Some other girls who were 12 and 13 were in a cabin near me and had said to me-- because that was something that was talked about, and I said, I don't have anything for seasickness-- and the girl said, well, if you need something we have Dramamine.
You know, come and get it.
Well, once the boat started this little girl was terribly sick, and I wasn't far behind.
And when we were ill that way mother used to put a little dab of 4711 cologne on our foreheads.
And this was to make you feel better.
Well, you know, if a little is good, well a lot has to be better.
And I must tell you, that cabin reeked from 4711 cologne.
But nothing helped.
And I went-- I wanted to go and find these girls with the medication.
And my timing was bad.
A steward was making the rounds of the boat and tried to tell me to go back to sleep and get into my cabin.
I didn't understand him and he didn't understand me.
I had a little bit of English.
I had tagged along with my sister to learn English, but it wasn't anything to understand.
Finally, the only way that he could make himself understood was grab my arm, shove me back into the cabin, and close the door.
All right, my mother said everything was going to be fine.
Nothing had been fine.
My mother was left on the platform, and I suddenly am on the train with people I don't know, and all these wonderful things are happening.
And I finally snuck out and I found the girls.
And they took us into their cabin.
They took the other little girl.
We got medicine into her and finally got her to sleep.
And in the morning we docked at Harwich.
And we were fed an English breakfast, white toast and tea.
Tea is fine, except that I'd never seen white bread before.
And adding up all the things that had happened-- because the steward really giving me a shove into the cabin I thought, you know, maybe this is not friendly.
And so I said to the kids around me when I saw this breakfast, that maybe we shouldn't eat this, because I think they're trying to poison us.
So, of course, a lot of the kids were even younger than me and looked at me like I was crazy.
And of course, I was.
We got off the boat.
And we were put into a huge room, told to get our luggage, and wait till our names to be called.
German.
At that point, German was used.
There were kids around me who were crying.
I had found my suitcase.
I remember telling them to sit on my suitcase and I would find theirs.
And we waited a few hours, I suppose.
And our names were called.
Our names being three others and mine.
And we were met by two gentlemen who also didn't speak German.
And we were told to go with them.
And we were-- we went with them.
And we went on a train.
The other three kids thought this was very funny, and were kind of rambunctious.
And I was being mother to them, and telling them they had to sit quietly, and they had to behave.
And we reached our destination, which was Kenilworth in the midlands of England, near a town of Coventry.
One of the gentlemen's name was Sidney Laxon.
He was a member of the Christadelphian ecclesia in Coventry.
Christadelphians are a sect of people, very fundamental, brothers in Christ.
Turns out that they established hostels for refugees.
And they took us into their homes.
Sidney Laxon was particularly benevolent, shall we say.
He had a lovely estate in Kenilworth.
And there was sort of the first glimmer that things were not going to be bad-- that they were OK.
He already had a refugee from Prague.
She had been with him a couple of months, spoke some English, translated for us, and said, in essence, it's OK guys, you're now fine.
Her name was Gretel.
And for some reason her last name has escaped me.
I've lost track of her.
She stayed in England.
She became a Christadelphian.
She became a nurse, and she stayed in England.
Her family was completely annihilated.
Once we were in Kenilworth for a couple of days, the four of us were then taken by Mr.
Laxon to homes that said they would take refugees.
So we went to the families, and I was taken by the first family we went to.
They were a young couple, about 28 years old.
They had a little boy who was 13 months old.
And I was chosen purely by my name.
They knew nothing about me.
These people knew nothing about us.
They volunteered to take us in.
I was chosen purely by my name.
Their little boy was named David.
Had it been a girl it would have been Suse.
The other three were taken in by three other families.
There were-- the Coventry ecclesia, or congregation, was about 100 families, I think.
Out of those 100, there were six of us that lived with Christadelphians in Coventry.
It's a big percentage when you think about it.
There were some others taken in in Coventry.
The cathedral sponsored a couple of people-- the Coventry cathedral-- and a couple of other people.
I stayed with the Parrys, George and Florence Parry.
They-- of course, immediately we wrote to my parents to let me know where I was, my address.
This proved to be very, very important later on.
And my foster father outlined to my father what would be taught to me as part of their religion, going to Sunday school or going with them to their services.
Of course, my father demurred.
I mean, you know, what was he going to do?
He wasn't going to agree.
This was not anything Jewish.
But, did he have a choice?
No.
I didn't start school until I knew a little bit of English.
My foster parents spoke no German.
And so my learning English was a lot on my own.
I taught him some German.
I did it by reading anything and everything that I could pick up with the English language, with a German-English Dictionary next to me.
I started school.
The English language is not a fun language to learn.
My first day in school, the-- it's a new term, and the teacher is reviewing and asking about times table.
I didn't know it was times table at the time.
It was tables.
Who knew?
And she goes from two to 12.
Well, after eight I became completely perplexed and very upset.
And when she was done she said to me-- you know, asked me what my problem was.
Well, to me a table was a table that you eat from.
And I figured that a table could be extended eight times, two leaves from each end, but not to 12.
So there was my first run-in with table and table in the English language.
I had news.
I had news.
Yes, we corresponded.
But of course, on the 1st of September, when war broke out, that's when it stopped.
And that's also when the neighbors-- and my foster parents had to deal with this-- the neighbors came to them and said, get rid of what you've got there in your house.
She's a Nazi.
And we don't want Nazis here.
And they had to explain that I was not a Nazi, they had rescued me from the Nazis.
But, of course, when I heard all of this, I retreated to the house and refused to go out until it was cleared up.
It took a couple of weeks until they convinced me.
War broke out.
The blitzkrieg started.
And on the 14th of November, Coventry was flattened.
My foster parents decided that they would have me evacuated.
And they found another family-- another Christadelphian family-- in the north of England who became my second set of foster parents.
They were a little bit older.
They had an older son.
They had a lovely home in the country.
And that's when I really began to love the country, and always hoped that some time I would be able to live in the country and not the city.
And many, many years later, in America, that came true.
Oh, yes.
Well, there wasn't much understanding, but I mean, I knew that the war was on.
And I knew that things were happening.
And because the Christadelphians are complete conscientious objectors, they do not fight.
They will go into the Medical Corps or something like this.
My foster father, for instance, during the war was a telephone operator at night.
When others went to war, this was his part of the war effort.
My foster parents did not want me to buy war bonds, or stamps, or whatever was that.
Their explanation was, number one, that of course, they didn't want me to do it because of their belief.
And the other thing that they said to me was, well, you don't want to buy war bonds that are going to buy ammunition to bomb your parents.
You see, so this was a rationalization that they also, that they gave me.
Yes, war was going on.
And when I heard nothing more from my parents, there was a concern.
Did I have parents?
Did I have a sister?
Nobody knew.
As far as the concentration camps were concerned, no, I did not know that until much later.
Also, it was still the era of keeping things from the children.
The children were not involved in family discussions, or discussions of bad things happening, as they are today.
We were not-- we were not as open.
So, you know, that was also another thing.
It was shielding me.
During this time, I cannot compare my life with any of the survivors, except that we had bombing in England.
We had severe rationing in England.
Food was at a premium.
I mean, there was, there was very, there was very little of it.
Bread was a, was a big mainstay.
Other things were gone.
I mean, there were no eggs.
There was no fruit except apples and pears in the summer time.
Vegetables in the winter were cabbage and carrots, because that was grown.
All the parks in England had no more flowers.
They had vegetable beds.
I just-- I just didn't-- I was-- I was always different.
I was much more serious than the kids in school, even, even in England.
I had more to think about.
I had more to worry about than, than playing and, you know, and just-- and being a child.
My foster brothers had, had grandparents, which became Gran and Gramps to me, too.
But it wasn't the same.
There was a, there was a big culture shock when you live in another country.
When I first got there, it was-- my foster mother didn't wash my hair the same way as my mother did.
It was big acclimation for both of us.
And this woman had tremendous patience.
And any of them, any of these people who took us in had tremendous patience.
You know, things happened to us.
I had a, I had a very, I had a dear girlfriend, who was a kindertransport, living with friends of my foster parents.
And her mother and aunt came to England as domestics.
Had no way of taking care of this girl, but literally kidnapped her from her foster parents and took her to London, and severed complete relationships with me as her friend, with the foster parents.
And the foster mother, I think, died of a broken heart.
Yes, she was ill, but a lot of it was psychosomatic.
She just never got over having Liesel snatched from her that way, leaving all of Liesel's clothes in the cupboards.
Liesel Baumgarten.
She was from Vienna.
You know, these kind of things.
And you lived with, with all sorts of fears and-- I had lots of friends.
But I was an avid reader all the time.
And I read the books from cover to cover.
Oh, the English-- oh all the time, all the time.
The relationship was fine.
I had lots of excellent friends.
Because I had birthday parties and, you know, I would go to people's houses-- girlfriends houses-- to, to play or to do things with them.
Oh yeah.
Oh, yes, it was always a curiosity.
I was always a curiosity.
And I did learn English quickly.
And for that I was sort of, from the teachers, made an example of.
You know, look how, look how well this little girl is doing.
You know, if somebody else wasn't doing well in class, which didn't sit very well with me.
That's why I've always, I'd always tried to lose the English accent later on-- tried to lose the German accent.
And little by little, German faded into almost nonexistence with me.
It did-- just nonexistence.
It was, was almost a mental block, so that it was only English.
The ones in Coventry.
I knew about three or four of them.
I still have-- no, no, no-- ah well, originally.
But I'm talking about the-- those that were, that were with the families in Coventry.
We were always in touch, because they were with Christadelphian families so we met at social situations.
Even the families socialized, too.
And so we met under those kind of situations.
But I had a lot of English friends all the time, both in the village of Cuddington where I stayed and in Coventry.
And the time was divided that I was in Coventry during the summer time, when the rains were not bad, and I was in the country in the winter time.
Always concerned.
I mean, the stuff that was in the newspapers-- and as I said, it was very vivid because we had the bombings, too.
And then the bombings in, in Germany that we, you know, knew about.
I mean, we knew as soon as Britain bombed Germany we were going to get it the next night.
There was just no question.
We knew exactly when we were going to get a big one, because it was, you know, tit for tat kind of thing that went on.
My foster mother's brother-in-law was part of D-day and got badly wounded.
D-day was June 6th, 1944, and I was 13 years old.
And I was following very, very avidly what was happening after that, of course, because then came the real onslaught into Germany.
As I said, eight-- of course, as I said, being an avid reader, I read the books.
And Anne was an orphan adopted by a couple, and I identified with her.
I did a lot of daydreaming about how things might be, shall we say.
And so--
Well, I was taken away from the foster home at the end of 1944, because Bloomsbury House in London decided that I was still around.
Bloomsbury House in London at that time had not bothered with me at all.
My foster father was the one who found out that the St.
John's Wood synagogue, the liberal synagogue in London, was doing correspondence courses in Jewish history, and registered me for that.
Had nothing to do with anybody else.
They tried to get a hold of anybody, any Jewish people in Coventry.
Nobody came forward, unfortunately.
My foster parents had tried.
But I did take the Jewish history correspondence, which, I have to tell you, most of it I already knew.
But as a child, of course, I wasn't happy about more homework to be done.
But I did it anyway.
Sure, the-- you know, the war and-- I mean, there was Italy.
And then of course we also had-- we had a prisoner of war camp not too far from us, where I lived in the country.
So war and its, all its ramifications were all around me all the time.
We-- but, of course, after June 6th, then it became more poignant because of it reaching Europe.
Now, what was going to happen?
Was I going to see my parents again?
Was I going to have parents?
How quickly were they going to get into Germany?
And were we going to win this war?
I mean, you know, Britain was threatened with war so many times, we had to carry gas masks all the time.
If we were caught without a gas mask, we were in trouble.
